title: The default mode network and rumination in PTSD

htmlContent:
The brain's background hum

I used to think my mind was broken. Not in the dramatic, movie-of-the-week way. More like a radio that couldn't stop scanning stations. Static, always static. You'd be sitting there, trying to read a book or have a conversation, and somewhere in the background your brain is running laps. Replaying something your boss said three years ago. Rehearsing an argument that hasn't happened. Scanning for threats in a room full of people who love you.

That's not laziness. That's not a character flaw. That's your default mode network on overdrive.

Here's the thing. The default mode network, or DMN, is a set of brain regions that light up when you're not focused on the outside world. When you're daydreaming, remembering, planning, worrying about the future. It's your brain's idling state. And for most people, it's relatively quiet. A gentle hum. But for those of us with PTSD, that hum turns into a roar.

Dan Siegel, a clinical professor of psychiatry at UCLA, has written extensively about how the DMN relates to our sense of self. He calls it part of the brain's "narrative circuitry." The part that tells you a story about who you are. And when that story is rooted in trauma, the DMN doesn't just tell it. It screams it. Over and over.

I remember sitting in my car in a parking lot, not even realizing I'd been there for forty-five minutes. My mind had been running through a loop. That thing I said in 2014. That look on someone's face. The shame, the guilt, the fear. All of it, on repeat. I wasn't choosing to think about it. It was happening to me.

Right? You know that feeling. The one where your brain feels like a haunted house you can't leave.

Rumination as a survival strategy

Here's what I didn't understand for a long time. Rumination isn't just a bad habit. It's not something you can "snap out of" with positive affirmations or a gratitude journal. It's a survival strategy that got stuck.

Bessel van der Kolk, the psychiatrist who wrote "The Body Keeps the Score," has spent decades showing how trauma reshapes the brain. He talks about how the brain's alarm system gets rewired after trauma. The amygdala becomes hypervigilant. The hippocampus shrinks. And the DMN? It gets stuck in a loop of threat assessment.

Think about it. In a dangerous environment, replaying past threats helps you predict future ones. If you grew up in chaos, your brain learned that scanning for danger was the price of survival. The problem is, that scanning never stops. Even when you're safe. Even when you're lying in bed at 2 AM, exhausted, desperate for sleep, your brain is still running the program. Because it doesn't know the danger is over.

A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many is "What Happened to You?" (paid link) by Oprah Winfrey and Dr. Bruce Perry. It shifts the question from "what's wrong with you" to "what happened to you." And that shift matters, because it changes how you relate to your own mind. Your DMN isn't defective. It's adaptive. It's just adapted to a world that no longer exists.

I've had clients tell me they feel like they're "addicted" to their own thoughts. And honestly, there's some truth to that. Rumination triggers a dopamine response. It gives you a sense of control, even if it's false control. Your brain would rather feel bad and feel in control than feel uncertain and out of control. So it keeps chewing on the same bone. Over and over.

Wild, right? Your own mind is trying to protect you, and it's making you miserable in the process.

The DMN and the body's alarm system

Here's where it gets really interesting. The default mode network doesn't operate in isolation. It's deeply connected to your nervous system. Stephen Porges, the neuroscientist who developed polyvagal theory, showed us that our state of safety or danger is registered in the body before the mind even knows what's happening.

So when your DMN is looping through traumatic memories, your body responds as if the threat is happening right now. Your heart rate increases. Your breathing gets shallow. Your muscles tense. And that physical state feeds back into the DMN, confirming that yes, there is danger. The loop tightens.

A resource that helps make polyvagal theory practical is "The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy" (paid link) by Deb Dana. It gives you concrete ways to recognize when your nervous system is in threat mode and how to gently guide it back toward safety. Because you can't talk your DMN out of its loop. You have to show your body that you're safe first.

I've noticed something in my own recovery. When I'm ruminating, trying to reason with myself doesn't work. "Stop thinking about that, it's not helpful" just makes me think about it more. But if I take a few slow breaths, if I put my hand on my chest, if I look around the room and name three things I can see, something shifts. The loop loosens. Not breaks, but loosens.

That's not spiritual woo. That's neuroscience. You're giving your ventral vagal system a chance to activate. And when your body feels safe, your DMN has permission to quiet down.

For those looking for gentle nervous system support, something worth trying is NOW L-Theanine 200mg (paid link). It's an amino acid that promotes relaxation without sedation. I've found it helpful for taking the edge off the mental chatter, especially at night when the DMN tends to get loudest.

Breaking the loop without breaking yourself

So how do you actually work with this? Not against it. Because fighting your own brain is exhausting, and it doesn't work anyway.

First, name what's happening. "Oh, that's my default mode network running a trauma loop." That simple act of labeling creates a tiny bit of distance. You're not the loop. You're the one noticing the loop. That's the beginning of what Dan Siegel calls "integration." Bringing online the prefrontal cortex, the part of your brain that can observe without getting swallowed.

Second, interrupt the physical state. The DMN is tied to your body. Change your body, and you change what the DMN has to work with. Stand up. Stretch. Splash cold water on your face. Take a walk. Do something that shifts your physiology. It doesn't have to be dramatic. Just different.

Third, be patient with the timeline. Your DMN has been running this program for years, maybe decades. It's not going to rewire overnight. Peter Levine, the developer of Somatic Experiencing, talks about "pendulation" - the natural rhythm of moving between activation and settling. You don't have to eliminate the rumination. You just have to let it move through you without getting stuck.

For those exploring somatic approaches, a book worth considering is "Waking the Tiger" (paid link) by Peter Levine. It explains how trauma gets trapped in the body and how gentle, titrated movement can help release it. It's not about catharsis or reliving the trauma. It's about completing the survival response that got interrupted.

I'm not going to tell you that you can "quiet your mind" completely. That's wellness industry nonsense. The DMN is always going to be there. It's part of being human. But you can learn to turn down the volume. You can learn to recognize when the loop is running and choose not to feed it.

Sit with that for a second. You don't have to believe every thought your brain generates. You can watch the thought arise, acknowledge it, and let it pass. Like clouds moving across the sky. You are the sky, not the clouds.

That's not easy. It's practice. And some days it doesn't work at all. But over time, the gaps between the loops get longer. The volume gets lower. The static becomes background noise instead of the main event.

Healing Journey Resources

  • "What Happened to You?" by Oprah Winfrey and Dr. Bruce Perry (paid link)
  • "The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy" by Deb Dana (paid link)
  • NOW L-Theanine 200mg (paid link)
  • "Waking the Tiger" by Peter Levine (paid link)
